¶

Predicting Breast Cancer Using Clinical Data¶

Author: Luis Rito Filipe¶

October, 2023¶


Summary¶

This study evaluates multiple machine learning models for predicting breast cancer using clinical data, with the primary objective of minimizing false negative diagnoses while maintaining false positives at an acceptable level.

Among the evaluated models, LightGBM was selected as the best-performing algorithm based on its superior recall, F1Score and MCC, while satisfying the defined constraints (Precision ≥ 0.6 and Recall ≥ 0.7). This selection aligns with the objective of minimizing false negatives while controlling the false positive rate.

The results demonstrate that breast cancer prediction is driven by a significative number of highly informative features, particularly Glucose, Resistin, Age, BMI and HOMA, with other variables providing complementary contributions.

Feature engineering had limited impact on model performance, suggesting that the original variables already capture most of the relevant information required for accurate classification.

Although the number of observations in this dataset may be considered relatively limited for drawing definitive clinical conclusions, some features provide sufficiently strong signals for class discrimination.

About The Study¶

The objective of this study is to compare different machine learning algorithms in predicting whether patients have breast cancer based on clinical data. To achieve this, widely used Python libraries for data analysis and machine learning are employed, including tools for data manipulation, visualization, and predictive modeling. The study follows a structured approach, beginning with exploratory data analysis (EDA), followed by data preprocessing and feature engineering, and concluding with model training, evaluation, and comparison. The goal is to identify which algorithms perform best under consistent evaluation criteria and to understand the key factors associated with breast cancer prediction.

About The Dataset¶

This study uses a dataset of clinical measurements provided by the UCI Machine Learning Repository (see link below). The dataset contains clinical data recorded by trained staff, with checks to ensure accuracy and completeness, including variables such as age, glucose concentration, body mass index (BMI) and other health indicators. The target variable indicates whether a patient was diagnosed with breast cancer.

Dataset link: https://archive.ics.uci.edu/dataset/451/breast+cancer+coimbra

Data Wrangling¶

The dataset contains nine independent variables and one target variable, named Classification.

Exploratory Data Analysis¶


One additional feature was engineered to capture non-linear relationships and enhance the predictive power of the models. This feature was designed to reflect meaningful interactions between clinical variables. The High_Glucose feature encodes elevated glucose levels using a nonlinear transformation above a threshold of 140, emphasizing the increased risk associated with higher glucose concentrations. This engineered feature aim to provide models with more informative signals than the original variables alone, particularly in capturing interactions and nonlinear effects.

Other engineered features such as Age_BMI and BMI_Glucose were tested and did not produce better results. Therefore, they were not included. The Classification dependent variable was renamed as Class.


Age BMI Glucose Insulin HOMA Leptin Adiponectin Resistin MCP.1 Class High_Glucose

25% 45.000000 22.973205 85.750000 4.359250 0.917966 12.313675 5.474283 6.881763 269.978250 0.000000 0.000000
50% 56.000000 27.662416 92.000000 5.924500 1.380939 20.271000 8.352692 10.827740 471.322500 1.000000 0.000000
75% 71.000000 31.241442 102.000000 11.189250 2.857787 37.378300 11.815970 17.755207 700.085000 1.000000 0.000000
max 89.000000 38.578759 201.000000 58.460000 25.050342 90.280000 38.040000 82.100000 1698.440000 1.000000 4.127134

The dataset exhibits a balanced dependent variable, with 55% of observations corresponding to positive breast cancer diagnoses. Feature correlation analysis was performed to identify highly correlated pairs of independent variables, enabling the removal of redundant features and improving model robustness.

A correlation threshold of 0.95 was adopted, such that when two independent variables exhibited an absolute correlation coefficient equal to or greater than this value, the feature with the lower mutual information score relative to the target variable was removed. This approach reduces feature redundancy while preserving the variables with the highest predictive relevance, mitigating multicollinearity effects and decreasing the risk of overfitting

In this dataset, including the engineered features, no pairs of independent variables were found to exceed the defined correlation threshold, as illustrated in the correlation matrix. This indicates that the feature set does not exhibit significant redundancy and can be retained without further dimensionality reduction.



The following chart illustrates the correlation between the target variable Class and the independent features. The results indicate that Glucose, HOMA and Insulin exhibit the strongest positive correlations with the target variable, suggesting that higher values of these features are associated with an increased likelihood of belonging to the positive class.

It is important to note that correlation measures linear relationships only and does not capture potential nonlinear interactions between variables. Therefore, features with low correlation may still contribute to model performance when combined with other variables or when nonlinear effects are considered.



The boxplot analysis reveals that Glucose, HOMA, Insulin and Resistin display a reduced interquartile overlap between classes. Despite a low variability, these characteristics indicate strong discriminative power and suggest that these features contribute significantly to class separability.



In the next step, the dataset is split into training and testing sets to allow proper training of the classification models and unbiased evaluation of their performance.


X_train.shape= (77, 10) Y_train.shape= (77,)
X_test.shape= (39, 10) Y_test.shape= (39,)

Model Comparison and Selection

The next step is to define the desired model performance objective. In the context of breast cancer diagnosis, minimizing false negatives is prioritized, as failing to identify positive cases has higher clinical risk, while a certain level of false positives is acceptable.

Model selection is therefore performed by first identifying all threshold configurations satisfying the predefined constraints on precision and recall (equal or higher than 0.6 and 0.7, respectively). Within this feasible set, the optimal threshold for each model was selected by maximizing the F1Score, ensuring a balanced trade-off between true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives. This approach guarantees that the selected operating point achieves both the desired sensitivity and an overall balanced classification performance.


Model Threshold PrecisionScore RecallScore F1Score TP TN FP FN MCC LogLoss BrierScoreLoss ROC_AUC PR_AUC
0 LightGBM 0.50 0.875000 0.954545 0.913043 21 14 3 1 0.793060 0.453376 0.132488 0.879679 0.845274
1 XGBoost 0.33 0.840000 0.954545 0.893617 21 13 4 1 0.743503 0.496507 0.151791 0.852941 0.810433
2 CatBoost 0.36 0.807692 0.954545 0.875000 21 12 5 1 0.694709 0.556657 0.146682 0.852941 0.826913
3 SVM 0.38 0.826087 0.863636 0.844444 19 13 4 3 0.633444 0.554708 0.186467 0.823529 0.854744
4 Random Forest 0.56 0.818182 0.818182 0.818182 18 13 4 4 0.582888 0.573698 0.190820 0.807487 0.799770
5 K-Nearest Neighbors 0.43 0.782609 0.818182 0.800000 18 12 5 4 0.528319 0.560493 0.188311 0.791444 0.839653
6 Logistic Regression 0.50 0.772727 0.772727 0.772727 17 12 5 5 0.478610 0.625918 0.218938 0.772727 0.851855
7 Multilayer Perceptron 0.03 0.739130 0.772727 0.755556 17 11 6 5 0.423194 2.581622 0.296877 0.786096 0.813748

The model comparison results indicate that several tree-based ensemble methods, including LightGBM, XGBoost and CatBoost, achieve identical RecallScore under the defined constraints. These models reach high recall while maintaining high precision and a relatively low number of false positives, resulting in strong PR_AUC scores.

This convergence in performance suggests that classes are separable. Among the top-performing models, LightGBM shows advantage in probabilistic calibration, as reflected by its lower LogLoss and Brier Score.

Overall, these results indicate that LightGBM is capable of achieving good classification performance, providing the most reliable probability estimates among all the models tested in this dataset.



In the score distribution plots, the horizontal axis represents the predicted probability of belonging to the positive class. A model with strong discriminative ability assigns low probabilities to negative cases (concentrated near 0) and high probabilities to positive cases (concentrated near 1).

The overlap between the two distributions corresponds to regions where both classes receive similar predicted probabilities, leading to potential misclassifications. This overlap is not limited to the intersection point of the curves but represents the entire shared area where both class densities are non-zero.

The score distribution plots below show a relatively good separation between classes considering the number of records in this dataset, with predicted probabilities for positive cases concentrated close to 1 and negative cases clustered at lower values. The overlap between the two distributions indicates good discriminative performance across all models. This suggests that the selected features provide high informative signals, enabling the models to distinguish between classes relatively well.



The SHAP summary plot indicates that breast cancer prediction is primarily driven by a subset of features, with Glucose and Resistin exhibiting the strongest overall impact on the model’s output, followed by Age, BMI and HOMA. Higher values of Glucose and Resistin consistently increase the predicted probability of breast cancer, while lower values are associated with a sharp decrease in predicted risk. Mixed values observed in Age, BMI and HOMA make it difficult to conclude about the contributions of these features.


Conclusion

Although the number of observations in this dataset may be considered relatively limited for drawing definitive clinical conclusions, the results indicate that the available features reliably capture relevant patient conditions and provide sufficiently strong signals for class discrimination.

The inclusion of engineered features did not substantially improve model performance in terms of positive case detection or false negative reduction. This suggests that the original variables already contain a high level of predictive information and that the additional engineered features introduced limited incremental value for this particular dataset.

Overall, the results indicate that model performance is primarily driven by a subset of highly informative variables, particularly Glucose and Resistin, while the remaining features contribute complementary but comparatively weaker predictive information. These findings suggest that the dataset possesses a good underlying signal structure, enabling a solid classification performance even with relatively simple feature representations.
